medications.indication_rationale: Short-course oral NSAID for symptomatic relief of the inflammatory articular pain of a mild lupus flare, as given in the source note. Provides analgesia while restarted hydroxychloroquine takes effect.
medications.dose_route_frequency: naproxen 500 mg orally, taken with food (dose per source note; dosing frequency not specified in source — confirm with prescriber)
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution/avoid if there is any suggestion of lupus nephritis or renal impairment — NSAIDs reduce renal perfusion and can worsen renal function; renal status is unknown remotely
Standard NSAID cautions: peptic ulcer disease, uncontrolled hypertension, heart failure, third-trimester pregnancy
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: NSAID plus possible undiagnosed lupus nephritis — nephrotoxic risk if renal involvement is present; the clinician flagged the need for prompt review before assuming renal safety

medications.indication_rationale: Restart the patient's regular anchor DMARD immediately — non-adherence over the past week precipitated this flare, and re-establishing baseline hydroxychloroquine is central to resolution and preventing recurrence.
medications.dose_route_frequency: resume the patient's usual maintenance dose (specific dose not stated in source; do not exceed the usual prescribed weight-based dose)
contraindications_in_this_case: Known hypersensitivity to 4-aminoquinolines; pre-existing retinopathy (routine ophthalmology monitoring applies but does not delay restart of an established maintenance medication)

medications.indication_rationale: Not to be initiated remotely for a mild articular flare — systemic steroids are reserved for moderate-to-severe or organ-involving flares assessed in person, and starting them via telehealth before infection and organ involvement are excluded risks masking sepsis and unnecessary immunosuppression. A short steroid course may be a rheumatology decision after review.

Do NOT prescribe antibiotics for presumed septic arthritis without examination and joint aspiration — empirical antibiotics both fail to treat an undrained septic joint and obscure the diagnosis. If septic arthritis is suspected the correct action is urgent in-person assessment, not a remote antibiotic script.

Opioids are not appropriate for an inflammatory articular flare — they do not treat inflammation, carry dependence and sedation risk in a patient living alone, and are not part of the source management. Simple analgesia plus NSAID and DMARD restart is the correct approach.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Resume hydroxychloroquine exactly as prescribed every day and set up a reminder/dosette system to prevent missed doses.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Missed doses precipitated this flare; consistent adherence is the single most effective way to prevent these 'crashes'.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a warm wheat pack to the most painful joints and rest tomorrow; monitor for the specific red-flag symptoms provided.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Local heat and rest give symptomatic relief for inflammatory joint pain while medication rebuilds; active symptom monitoring supports safe self-management at home.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Maintain good oral fluid intake and keep water by the bedside.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Supports general recovery and, given NSAID use, helps maintain renal perfusion; the patient already has water bedside.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a flare of your lupus affecting the joints and your energy, most likely set off by the missed tablets — not damage to your heart, lungs or kidneys tonight based on what you've told me; but because I can't examine you over chat I can't be certain, which is why you need to be seen soon.
The anti-inflammatory tablet manages the pain while your regular lupus medication builds back up in your system over the coming days.
Restarting your hydroxychloroquine every day is the key to settling this flare and preventing the next one.
Rest tomorrow, use warm packs on the sore joints, and drink plenty of fluids.
Because you live alone and are very fatigued, keep your phone within reach and let someone know how you are tonight.

follow_up_plan.interval: within 48-72 hours (sooner if any red-flag symptom appears)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: flare resolution, in-person joint examination to exclude a septic joint, cardiac/chest auscultation, urinalysis and bloods for organ involvement, and medication adherence; rheumatology review as needed
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any pleuritic chest pain, breathlessness, high fever, a single hot red swollen joint, or cola/tea-coloured urine — seek urgent care immediately rather than waiting for the scheduled review

must_include_items: Recognise a probable articular/constitutional lupus flare rather than escalating to emergency services for a stable presentation
Elicit the patient-reportable red-flag negatives (chest pain, breathlessness, dark/cola urine, high fever, single hot red joint)
Identify missed hydroxychloroquine as the likely precipitant and advise restarting it immediately
Advise symptomatic relief (simple/NSAID analgesia as appropriate, warm packs, rest, fluids)
Arrange PROMPT (24-48h) in-person GP/rheumatology review because organ involvement and septic arthritis cannot be excluded remotely
Give explicit red-flag safety-netting that steps up to urgent care/ED (and 000 if severe) if warning symptoms appear
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending paracetamol as first-line analgesia instead of, or alongside, an NSAID (reasonable given unknown renal status)
Arranging urgent same-day (T3) face-to-face review rather than 24-48h if the clinician judges the inability to examine plus living-alone context warrants it
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any in-person review / signing off from home
Failing to screen for the organ-involvement and septic-joint red flags
Failing to address medication adherence / restarting hydroxychloroquine
Failing to provide safety-netting escalation advice
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a stable articular flare to 000/ambulance or immediate ED
Claiming to have excluded pericarditis, pleuritis, nephritis or septic arthritis remotely
Prescribing systemic corticosteroids remotely for a mild flare
Prescribing empirical antibiotics for presumed septic arthritis without examination/aspiration
Prescribing an NSAID without cautioning about possible renal involvement, or reaching for opioids
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) not over-triaging a stable flare AND (b) arranging prompt in-person review with explicit red-flag safety-netting AND (c) addressing hydroxychloroquine restart. Confidently excluding the dangerous mimics remotely, or escalating to 000 for this stable presentation, are both failing responses.
